name: Training vs. Inference
definition: Training is when a model learns patterns from data. Inference is when the trained model is used to generate outputs.
my_take: This distinction matters because a lot of AI-risk arguments blur what happens during training with what happens during deployment.
why_it_matters: If we are talking about alignment, capability, or governance, we need to know whether the risk emerges during training, inference, or the surrounding system.

name: Loss Function / Objective Function vs. Reward Signal
definition: A loss or objective function is what the system is optimized against. A reward signal is feedback used to shape behavior, especially in reinforcement learning.
my_take: This is central to the book: what we optimize for is not necessarily what we actually want.
why_it_matters: A proxy objective can produce behavior that looks right while missing the underlying value.

sources.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences
Learning to Summarize from Human Feedback

name: Gradient Descent
definition: Gradient descent is an optimization method that gradually adjusts model parameters to reduce error.
my_take: For this project, the important point is not the math derivation. It is that intelligence can emerge from an optimization process rather than being explicitly programmed.
why_it_matters: This is the foundation of the book's 'grown, not crafted' argument.

sources.title: Neural Networks and Deep Learning — Michael Nielsen
Deep Learning — Optimization for Training Deep Models

name: RLHF
definition: Reinforcement learning from human feedback uses human preferences to train models toward outputs people rate as better.
my_take: RLHF is useful, but it is still a proxy. It trains behavior toward what humans reward, not necessarily toward truth, safety, or genuine alignment.
why_it_matters: This connects directly to the book's claim that training for one thing may produce something else.

sources.title: Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback
Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback

name: Overfitting vs. Generalization
definition: Overfitting happens when a model memorizes patterns too narrowly. Generalization is when it performs well on new examples.
my_take: Generalization is where the interesting—and sometimes scary—behavior appears. A model that generalizes can apply strategies outside the exact context it was trained on.
why_it_matters: This matters for agency, transfer, jailbreaks, and unexpected capability jumps.

sources.title: Understanding Deep Learning Requires Rethinking Generalization

name: Embeddings / Latent Space
definition: Embeddings are numerical representations of concepts. Latent space is the learned internal space where relationships between concepts are represented.
my_take: This is where the 'gray box' intuition starts. We can inspect the vectors, but their meaning is distributed and not always human-legible.
why_it_matters: Latent representations are central to why models can generalize, reason by similarity, and develop internal abstractions.

name: Black Box vs. Gray Box vs. White Box
definition: A black box is only observable from inputs and outputs. A white box is fully interpretable. A gray box is inspectable but not fully understood.
my_take: LLMs are gray boxes, not black boxes. We can inspect weights and activations, but we do not yet have a complete mechanistic theory.
why_it_matters: This is one of my main pushbacks on the book's framing.

name: Scaling Laws
definition: Scaling laws describe predictable relationships between model performance, data, compute, and model size.
my_take: Scaling laws help explain why capability can appear to jump discontinuously from a human perspective, even if the underlying curve is smoother.
why_it_matters: They are important for understanding why governance and alignment can lag behind capability.

sources.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
